stem: The region in the first quadrant bounded by the line \( y = x + [[c]] \), the horizontal line \( y = [[k]] \), and the \( y \)-axis is revolved about the \( x \)-axis. Find the volume of the solid generated.
solution: Using washers perpendicular to the \( x \)-axis, the outer radius is \( [[k]] \) and the inner radius is \( x + [[c]] \), for \( 0 \le x \le [[k]]-[[c]] \). The volume is \( \pi\int_{0}^{[[k]]-[[c]]}\left([[k]]^{2}-(x+[[c]])^{2}\right)dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This squares the difference of the radii instead of taking the difference of the squares.
This drops the factor of \( \pi \).
This uses only the outer radius (a solid disk), ignoring the hole.
This doubles the correct volume.
This is off by \( \pi \) from the correct volume.

stem: The region in the first quadrant under the curve \( y = \sqrt{[[k]]x} \), from \( x = [[a]] \) to \( x = [[b]] \), is revolved about the \( x \)-axis. Find the volume of the solid generated.
solution: Since there is no hole, each disk perpendicular to the \( x \)-axis has radius \( y=\sqrt{[[k]]x} \), so its area is \( \pi y^{2} = \pi [[k]]x \). The volume is \( \pi\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}[[k]]x\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the radius directly (not squared) times the width, forgetting to integrate \( x \).
This drops the factor of \( \pi \).
This squares the coefficient \( [[k]] \) instead of just the radius.
This doubles the correct volume.
This is off by \( \pi \) from the correct volume.

stem: The region in the first quadrant bounded by the line \( x = y + [[c]] \), the vertical line \( x = [[k]] \), and the \( x \)-axis is revolved about the \( y \)-axis. Find the volume of the solid generated.
solution: Using washers perpendicular to the \( y \)-axis, the outer radius is \( [[k]] \) and the inner radius is \( y + [[c]] \), for \( 0 \le y \le [[k]]-[[c]] \). The volume is \( \pi\int_{0}^{[[k]]-[[c]]}\left([[k]]^{2}-(y+[[c]])^{2}\right)dy = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This squares the difference of the radii instead of taking the difference of the squares.
This drops the factor of \( \pi \).
This uses only the outer radius (a solid disk), ignoring the hole.
This doubles the correct volume.
This is off by \( \pi \) from the correct volume.

stem: The region in the first quadrant to the left of the curve \( x = \sqrt{[[k]]y} \), from \( y = [[p]] \) to \( y = [[q]] \), is revolved about the \( y \)-axis. Find the volume of the solid generated.
solution: Since there is no hole, each disk perpendicular to the \( y \)-axis has radius \( x=\sqrt{[[k]]y} \), so its area is \( \pi x^{2} = \pi [[k]]y \). The volume is \( \pi\int_{[[p]]}^{[[q]]}[[k]]y\,dy = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the radius directly (not squared) times the width, forgetting to integrate \( y \).
This drops the factor of \( \pi \).
This squares the coefficient \( [[k]] \) instead of just the radius.
This doubles the correct volume.
This is off by \( \pi \) from the correct volume.
